evidence_basis_summary: Authors apply explicit inclusion criteria C1-C5 (field, DOI, reproduction package, credible report, <2hr) and 4 source-specific filters to select 112 of many candidate papers, showing human-led adjudication of the benchmark universe.
notes: upgraded from low to high; Section 3.1 documents human inclusion criteria and cross-verified annotation; changed observable from no to partial and status from not_applicable to conditional because relevance work is explicit but confined to benchmark curation

evidence_basis_summary: Paper frames reproducibility as a critical-reasoning task requiring humans to adjudicate discrepancies between reported and reproduced results; agents score only 21.4% accuracy and REPRO-Agent only 36.6%, so humans still discipline mechanism/claim consistency.
notes: upgraded from low to high; Sections 2 and 5 describe critical reasoning and low agent accuracy as evidence humans still discipline claim-mechanism fit

evidence_basis_summary: Authors operationalize reproducibility as a 4-level ordinal construct (scores 1-4) finer than the SSRP 1-10 scale, defining category boundaries via consensus-based annotation with a legal expert.
notes: upgraded from low to high; Section 3.2 shows deliberate human-defined construct boundaries for reproducibility scoring

evidence_basis_summary: Benchmark deliberately combines 4 sources (mass-reproduction study, I4R, Retraction Watch, Twitter/X) and multiple languages (Stata, R, MATLAB, Julia, Python) and data formats to avoid collapsing to one machine-friendly corpus.
notes: upgraded from low to high; Section 3.1 and Table 1 document deliberate multi-source multi-language corpus design

evidence_basis_summary: Authors design the full assessment protocol: task formulation, scoring rubric, inclusion criteria, environment setup (Stata/MATLAB/LaTeX preinstalled), output file conventions, and cost cutoffs, matching the codebook's protocol-architect exemplar for case 27.
notes: confidence retained as high with direct evidence from Sections 3-4

evidence_basis_summary: Paper addresses reproducibility (same data, same methods) not replicability or transportability; Section 2 explicitly distinguishes reproducibility from replicability and excludes cross-setting transport.
notes: upgraded from low to high; Section 2 directly rules transportability out of scope

evidence_basis_summary: Benchmark operates entirely on archival paper PDFs and reproduction packages with no fieldwork or interview component.
notes: upgraded from low to high; full-text review confirms no situated/fieldwork dimension

evidence_basis_summary: Named authorship across UIUC, SJTU, and Chicago; consensus-based 5-member annotation team including a legal expert; explicit cost caps ($4/task) and manual ground-truth labeling show humans bear accountability for benchmark validity.
notes: confidence retained as high; Sections 3.1-3.2 document authorship and consensus review

evidence_basis_summary: Authors explicitly define 4 reproducibility score boundaries (1 irreproducible; 2 minor code inconsistency; 3 reporting issues; 4 fully reproducible) with worked examples, a pure construct-boundary task.
notes: upgraded from low to high; Section 3.2 gives explicit category-boundary definitions

evidence_basis_summary: Annotation process targets full consensus with a single deterministic score per paper; authors explicitly state reproducibility is an objective, consensus-reachable task, so subgroup disagreement is not preserved.
notes: upgraded from low to high; Section 3.2 states consensus-based single-score design rules out perspective sampling

evidence_basis_summary: No cross-population or cross-setting transport is attempted; the task is computational reproduction on the original data only.
notes: upgraded from low to high; Section 2 confirms scope restricted to same-data reproducibility

evidence_basis_summary: Entire paper is a benchmarking/calibration exercise: human-annotated ground-truth reproducibility scores calibrate three agents (AutoGPT, CORE-Agent, SWE-Agent) and REPRO-Agent against expert judgment.
notes: upgraded from low to high and observable from no to yes; Sections 4-5 show the case is fundamentally a calibration architecture with humans setting the reference distribution

evidence_basis_summary: No interviewer or fieldworker role; benchmark is purely document-based.
notes: upgraded from low to high based on full-text confirmation

evidence_basis_summary: A legal expert with deep familiarity with social science documentation participates in cross-verification, providing tacit domain context for annotation.
notes: upgraded from low to high; Sections 3.1 and 3.2 explicitly invoke legal-expert contextual familiarity; changed observable from no to partial and status from not_applicable to conditional

evidence_basis_summary: Case does not involve title/abstract triage or early ranking; inclusion was done by humans applying structured criteria, not by AI screening.
notes: upgraded from low to high; full-text review shows no screening-automation dimension

role_label: Routine coder on high-consensus, ground-truth-rich tasks
evidence_basis_summary: Reproducibility scoring is explicitly a ground-truth-rich ordinal labeling task (112 papers, deterministic 1-4 scoring) that authors propose agents should automate; current agent accuracy (21-37%) shows the routine executor function is targeted for delegation but humans remain auditors.
notes: upgraded from low to high and observable from no to yes; the benchmark is designed precisely to test whether routine reproducibility coding can be delegated to agents with humans as auditors

evidence_basis_summary: Paper does not discuss AI drafting or prose polishing; task is reproducibility assessment not writing.
notes: upgraded from low to high; full-text review confirms no drafting/polishing dimension
